question_title: Will SpaceX launch Starship Flight 13 on or before June 14, 2026?
summary: Starship Flight 13 launching by June 14, 2026 is near-impossible. The FAA mishap investigation into Flight 12 was formally opened only 9 days ago (May 27) and remains active with no indication of imminent closure. No Starship-class investigation has ever closed in under 1 month — the fastest comparable case (New Glenn) took ~1 month, and prior Starship investigations took 2.5-5 months. Even if the FAA miraculously cleared SpaceX tomorrow, the hardware isn't ready: Ship 40 is targeting a mid-June static fire and Booster 20 is lagging behind. A YES outcome requires the unprecedented conjunction of record-fast investigation closure AND secretly ready hardware AND compressed pre-launch operations — all within 9 days. I assign 2% to account for the vanishingly small possibility of an expedited FAA return-to-flight determination combined with hidden hardware readiness, but this remains at the floor of plausibility.

factors.description: FAA mishap investigation is open with no closure announced (9 days since formal determination on May 27). FAA explicitly stated Starship cannot fly until investigation closes and corrective actions are approved.

Shortest comparable investigation took ~1 month (New Glenn); Starship Flight 8 took ~2.5 months; IFT-1 took 5 months with 63 corrective actions. Only 9 days remain — would require unprecedented speed.
Hardware not ready independently of FAA: Ship 40 targeting mid-June static fire, Booster 20 lagging further behind. Even without the regulatory hold, hardware wouldn't be launch-ready by June 14.
Novel V3/Raptor 3 hardware on its debut flight complicates root cause analysis — first flight of new engine design means investigation cannot rely on prior engine failure databases.
Expedited return-to-flight determination pathway exists (used for Flight 9 after Flight 8 mishap). IPO pressure and political leverage provide motivation for speed.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 2% (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**

This question is nearly certain to resolve NO. Three independent barriers make a Flight 13 launch by June 14 effectively impossible:

1. **FAA investigation still open** — Formally opened just 9 days ago (May 27). No closure or return-to-flight determination has been announced. SpaceX *cannot legally launch* until this clears.

2. **Historical precedent** — The fastest comparable investigation (New Glenn) took ~1 month. Starship Flight 8's investigation took ~2.5 months. IFT-1 took 5 months with 63 corrective actions. Closing in 9 days would be completely unprecedented.

3. **Hardware not ready** — Ship 40 is targeting a mid-June *static fire* (not launch), and Booster 20 is lagging behind even that. Even without the FAA hold, the vehicle wouldn't be launch-ready.

The only conceivable YES path requires all three barriers to dissolve simultaneously — an unprecedented rapid FAA determination, secretly ready hardware, and compressed operations — a conjunction so improbable it warrants only the minimum ~2% probability.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will SpaceX launch Starship Flight 13 on or before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43782)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if SpaceX initiates a launch attempt of Starship Flight 13 (Integrated Flight Test 13, the next full-stack Starship/Super Heavy launch from Starbase after Flight 12 of May 22, 2026) in which the vehicle lifts off the pad (clears the launch mount under its own power) at any time from 00:00 UTC on May 31, 2026 through 23:59 UTC on June 14, 2026. The launch attempt counts whether or not the mission succeeds. Resolution will be based on the Wikipedia 'List of Starship launches' page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Starship_launches), which records each flight's launch date and outcome. If Wikipedia has not been updated, resolution may instead use [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the liftoff. The question resolves NO if no Starship Flight 13 liftoff occurs in that window (e.g., the vehicle remains grounded by the FAA mishap investigation, hardware is not ready, or only static fires / wet dress rehearsals occur without a launch attempt).
fine_print: A 'liftoff' means the integrated Starship/Super Heavy vehicle physically leaves the launch mount under thrust. Aborts before engine ignition, static fires, and wet dress rehearsals do not count. A liftoff followed by an immediate explosion still counts as a launch for resolution purposes. If SpaceX flies a Starship mission from Starbase that it does not officially label 'Flight 13' but is unambiguously the next integrated flight test after Flight 12, that flight counts.

factors.description: FAA mishap investigation is open with no closure announced (9 days since formal determination). FAA explicitly stated Starship cannot fly until investigation closes.
Shortest historical Starship investigation was ~1 month (New Glenn precedent); Flight 8 took ~2.5 months; IFT-1 took 5 months. Only 9 days remain in resolution window.
Hardware not ready: Ship 40 targeting mid-June static fire, Booster 20 lagging. Even without FAA hold, hardware wouldn't be ready by June 14.
Novel V3/Raptor 3 hardware complicates root cause analysis - this was the first flight of new engine design, making investigation more complex
Possible expedited return-to-flight determination pathway exists (used for Flight 9 after Flight 8). SpaceX IPO pressure creates motivation.

input.anchor: Historical base rate: 0% - No Starship mishap investigation has ever closed in under 1 month, and there are only 9 days in the remaining window.
input.assessment:
This question asks whether Starship Flight 13 will launch by June 14, 2026. The answer is almost certainly NO for multiple independent reasons:

1. REGULATORY BLOCK: The FAA investigation is open as of today (June 5), only 9 days after the formal mishap determination. There is zero indication of imminent closure. The fastest any Starship investigation has ever been resolved is approximately 1 month (and that was New Glenn, not Starship). SpaceX cannot legally launch until this clears.

2. HARDWARE NOT READY: Even if the FAA magically cleared SpaceX tomorrow, the hardware isn't ready. Ship 40 is targeting a mid-June static fire (which would itself take time before launch), and Booster 20 is reportedly lagging behind that timeline.

3. HISTORICAL PRECEDENT: No Starship mishap investigation has ever closed in less than 1 month. The resolution window closes in ~9 days. This would require the investigation to close in record time AND hardware to be ready, which are two independent near-impossibilities.

The only conceivable YES scenario requires: (a) FAA issues an unprecedented rapid return-to-flight determination in days, AND (b) hardware is secretly ready despite public reports suggesting otherwise, AND (c) SpaceX performs all pre-launch operations in record time. The conjunction of these three improbabilities makes YES essentially impossible.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is near-zero based on historical precedent. The question has a very short time horizon (9 days) vs. multiple months for the fastest comparable investigation. Hardware readiness is an independent constraint that also isn't met. This is an extreme case where the evidence strongly supports NO.
input.key_uncertainties: The only uncertainty is whether the FAA could use an unprecedented expedited process. However, even this requires hardware readiness, which appears to not exist.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Provided excellent detailed information on investigation timelines and current status. Web search: Confirmed investigation is ongoing with no closure. Metaculus question fetch: Confirmed 0 forecasters, no community prediction available. All tools functioned well.
input.update_triggers: 1. FAA announces investigation closure or return-to-flight determination, 2. SpaceX announces launch date for Flight 13 in the next 9 days, 3. Reports of secret hardware readiness
input.process_reflection: This was a straightforward question where the evidence is overwhelmingly one-directional. The research tool provided comprehensive data on investigation timelines. The main challenge was that the research sub-agent's first query couldn't access web tools, but the second query succeeded. The process worked well for this type of clear-cut question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**All five factors were checked against the research trace.**

**Factor 1 (FAA investigation open, logit=-3.0):** Fully verified. The worldview research entry and multiple web search results (SpaceNews, Spaceflight Now, Space.com, Bloomberg) confirm the FAA formally classified Flight 12 as a mishap on May 27, 2026, and the investigation remains open as of June 5. The FAA quote is directly supported in the research.

**Factor 2 (Historical investigation durations, logit=-2.5):** Mostly verified. The research entry confirms IFT-1 took ~5 months, Flight 8 took ~2.5 months, and New Glenn took ~1 month. Minor quibble: the factor description says "Shortest historical Starship investigation was ~1 month (New Glenn precedent)" — New Glenn is Blue Origin's rocket, not Starship. However, the core claim (no comparable launch vehicle investigation has closed in under 1 month, let alone 9 days) is well-grounded and the conclusion is unchanged.

**Factor 3 (Hardware not ready, logit=-2.0):** Sourced from the Metaculus question's own background info ("Ship 40 is reportedly in final integration with a mid-June static fire target, while Booster 20 is lagging"). Reasonable source; the research tool's web capabilities were limited for independent verification but no contradictory evidence was found.

**Factor 4 (Novel V3/Raptor 3 hardware, logit=-1.0):** Verified. Research confirms Flight 12 was the first V3 flight with Raptor 3 engines. The research explicitly notes this novelty "may complicate root-cause analysis."

**Factor 5 (Expedited pathway exists, logit=+0.5):** Verified. Research confirms the return-to-flight determination pathway was used for Flight 9 after Flight 8. The low logit (+0.5) and very low confidence (0.3) appropriately discount this unlikely scenario.

**No double-counting detected:** Factors 1 and 2 both reference the FAA investigation but address different dimensions (current status vs. historical duration precedent). Not redundant.

**No wrong-direction factors:** All negative factors correctly have negative logits; the lone positive factor correctly has a positive logit.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** ✅ The agent correctly understood the resolution criteria (liftoff by June 14, 2026) and all factors address the key barriers.

**Counterargument quality:** Adequate. The counterargument constructs a plausible alternative scenario involving political leverage, IPO pressure, and potentially secret hardware readiness. It identifies four specific "what would change my mind" indicators.

**Probability assessment (2%):** Appropriate. The question requires conjunction of multiple near-impossible events within 9 days: unprecedented rapid FAA investigation closure AND hardware readiness despite reports to the contrary AND completion of all pre-launch operations. My independent estimate would be 1-2%, consistent with the agent's 2%.

**Confidence (0.92):** Appropriate given the strength and multiplicity of evidence sources.

**Tool audit:** The agent's narrative is consistent with ground truth metrics (5 calls, 0 errors). The first research query had limited web access but the agent appropriately compensated with the second query and web searches.

**No worldview contradictions found:** The worldview research entries on the FAA investigation are consistent with the factors.
